questions.question: Explain the difference between flow control and congestion control in TCP/IP networks.
keyPoints: Flow control manages data rate between specific sender-receiver pair using receiver window size
Congestion control manages data rate to prevent network overload
Flow control uses TCP header's window size field to advertise available buffer space
Congestion control uses algorithms like slow start, congestion avoidance, and fast retransmit
Both mechanisms limit the sender's transmission rate but at different levels (end-to-end vs network-wide)

questions.question: Calculate the efficiency of a Stop-and-Wait ARQ system with a frame size of 1000 bits and a one-way propagation delay of 25 ms on a 1 Mbps link.
keyPoints: Total time for one cycle = Frame transmission time + 2 × Propagation delay + Processing time
Frame transmission time = 1000 bits / 1 Mbps = 1 ms
Round trip delay = 2 × 25 ms = 50 ms
Total cycle time = 1 + 50 = 51 ms
Efficiency = Transmission time / Total cycle time = 1/51 ≈ 1.96%
Shows why Stop-and-Wait is inefficient for high-speed or high-latency networks

questions.question: Describe the working of the Sliding Window Protocol with a sender window size of 5.
keyPoints: Sender can transmit up to 5 frames without receiving acknowledgment
Each frame is assigned a sequence number
Window slides forward (increments) when ACK is received
Window establishes a pipeline of unacknowledged frames in transit
Increases link utilization compared to Stop-and-Wait
Receiver maintains own window to track expected frames

questions.question: What is the significance of the TCP Window Scale option?
questions.key: Explain how TCP Window Scaling extends the window size field and why it is needed.
keyPoints: Original TCP header has 16-bit window field, maximum 65,535 bytes
With high bandwidth-delay products (like modern networks), this limits throughput
Window Scale option allows window size to be shifted left by specified bits
Can advertise windows up to 1 GB (65,535 × 2^14)
Enabled during TCP 3-way handshake via scale factor option
Essential for utilizing full capacity of high-speed networks like 10 Gbps links

questions.question: Compare Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ protocols, highlighting their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Go-Back-N: Retransmits all frames after lost frame; simpler receiver, more bandwidth waste on retransmission
Selective Repeat: Retransmits only lost frame; complex receiver (buffering out-of-order), efficient bandwidth usage
Go-Back-N suitable for low error rates; Selective Repeat for high error rates
Go-Back-N requires fewer sequence number bits; Selective Repeat needs more to avoid ambiguity
Selective Repeat window size limited to 2^(n-1); Go-Back-N limited to 2^n - 1

questions.question: A TCP connection has a receiver window (rwnd) of 64 KB and the current congestion window (cwnd) is 32 KB. The round trip time is 100 ms. Calculate: (a) What is the effective window size? (b) What is the maximum throughput achievable? (c) If the RTT increases to 200 ms due to network congestion, what happens to throughput?
keyPoints: (a) Effective window = min(rwnd, cwnd) = min(64KB, 32KB) = 32 KB
(b) Throughput = Window size / RTT = 32 KB / 100 ms = 32 × 1024 × 8 / 0.1 = 2,621,440 bps ≈ 2.62 Mbps
(c) With RTT = 200 ms: Throughput = 32 KB / 200 ms = 32 × 1024 × 8 / 0.2 = 1,310,720 bps ≈ 1.31 Mbps
Throughput is inversely proportional to RTT
This demonstrates why latency affects throughput significantly
In practice, TCP would trigger congestion control and reduce cwnd further

questions.question: Design a flow control mechanism for a wireless network with high error rates and variable latency. Justify your design choices.
keyPoints: Need Selective Repeat ARQ rather than Go-Back-N due to high error rates
Variable latency requires adaptive timeout calculations using exponential weighted moving average
Smaller initial window size to prevent buffer overflow during bad conditions
Implement positive acknowledgments with individual sequence numbers
Add push flag for urgent data to bypass normal flow control
Consider using smaller window sizes to reduce overhead from retransmissions
Implement channel estimation and adaptive modulation to improve reliability
May need to incorporate explicit congestion notification (ECN)
Justification: High error rates make Go-Back-N inefficient; variable latency makes fixed timeouts unreliable
